Furthermore, the "Jinguji Documents" held by the former head temple ({{ill|Head temple|ja|別当寺}}) {{ill|Jinguji|ja|神宮寺 (千葉県神崎町)|lt=Jinguji|lt_ja=神宮寺}}, despite missing 60 out of 600 volumes of the {{ill|Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra|ja|大般若波羅蜜多経}}, are highly valued as a sutra transcription from the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|lt=Nanboku-chō period|lt_ja=南北朝期}}. The colophon ({{ill|Colophon|ja|奥書}}) reveals details about the temples ({{ill|Temple|ja|寺院}}), monks ({{ill|Monk|ja|僧侶}}), and the sphere of religious influence at that time, and it has also been designated as a tangible cultural property of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Tangible Cultural Property of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#古文書|lt=Chiba Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property|lt_ja=千葉県の有形文化財}}). == Transportation == * About a 20‐minute walk from {{ill|Narita Line|ja|成田線}}{{ill|Shimōsa-Kozaki Station|ja|下総神崎駅}} * Near the south end of Kozaki Ōhashi on {{ill|National Route 356|ja|国道356号}} and {{ill|Ibaraki and Chiba Prefectural Road 107, Edosaki-Kozaki Line|ja|茨城県道・千葉県道107号江戸崎神崎線}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Kenichi Tanikawa|ja|谷川健一}} ''The Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – Volume 11: Kanto'' {{ill|Hakusuisha|ja|白水社}}, 1984. ISBN 4-560-02221-6== See Also == {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|Kozaki Manor|ja|神崎荘}} == External Links == * [http://www.town.kozaki.chiba.jp/05kanko/history.html Kozaki Town History and Cultural Properties] * [http://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/7528/ Chiba Tourism Complete Guide: Great Kozaki Kusunoki (Kozaki Town)] {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Kozaki Town]] [[Category:History of Kozaki Town]] [[Category:Shrines of National Historic Significance]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:神崎神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also ==" to " == See Also! =="
revision.text:
{{ill|Kōzaki Shrine|ja|神崎神社}} [[Category:Translated Shrines and Temples]] [[Category:Shortest 250 Pages]] [[Category:As of 2025-02-19]] [[sn:ōzaki Shrine]] [[en:Kōzaki Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]] [[sn:ōzaki Shrine]] [[Category:11]] {{Otheruses|Kōzaki Shrine in Chiba Prefecture|Shrine in Kotoura, Tottori Prefecture|Kōzaki Shrine (Kotoura)}} {{Shinto shrine |名称= Kōzaki Shrine |image=[[File:2024-kasai_Kozaki_Shrine_and_Kozaki_no_O-kusu_Katori-JPN.jpg|250px|{{ill|Kōzaki Shrine|ja|神崎神社}}
Worship Hall and the Nanjamonja tree]] |所在地= {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}{{ill|Katori District|ja|香取郡}}{{ill|Kozaki Town|ja|神崎町}} Kozaki Honjuku 1994 |位置= {{coord|35|54|20|N|140|24|0|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |祭神= {{ill|Tennichibune no Mikoto|ja|天鳥船命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|lt=Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|lt_ja=大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|スクナビコナ|lt=Sukunahikona no Mikoto|lt_ja=少彦名命}} |社格= Prefectural shrine |創建= |本殿= {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} |別名= Komatsu Shrine |例祭= |神事= }} '''Kōzaki Shrine''' (神崎神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}{{ill|Katori District|ja|香取郡}}{{ill|Kozaki Town|ja|神崎町}} ({{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}}{{ill|Katori District|ja|香取郡}}). Its former {{ill|Shrine rank|ja|社格}} was defined under the {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度#諸社|lt=Prefectural shrine|lt_ja=県社}}. It enshrines as its main deities {{ill|Tennichibune no Mikoto|ja|天鳥船命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|lt=Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|lt_ja=大己貴命}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|スクナビコナ|lt=Sukunahikona no Mikoto|lt_ja=少彦名命}}. == Overview == The founding date of this shrine is unknown, but according to its tradition, it is said that in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Hakuhō|ja|白鳳}} era ({{ill|673|ja|673}}) it was relocated to this site from the two mounds at Ōura Pond, located on the border between {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国|lt=Hitachi|lt_ja=常陸}} and Shimōsa. Until the {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} period, it was known as "Komatsu Shrine", but in the 3rd year of the {{ill|Genkei|ja|元慶}} era ({{ill|879|ja|879}}) it was conferred the lower Junior Fifth Rank ({{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}}下) in the {{ill|Shinto ranking system|ja|神階}}.In the ''{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}'', in the entry for April 5 in the 3rd year of the Genkei era, it is recorded: "To the {{ill|Mihosu Shrine|ja|御穂神社|lt=Miryōjin|lt_ja=御廊神}} of Suruga Province with the upper Junior Fifth Rank, the lower Senior Fifth Rank is conferred, and to the Komatsu Shrine of Shimōsa Province with the upper Sixth Rank, the lower Junior Fifth Rank is conferred." This shrine is not an auxiliary shrine ({{ill|Auxiliary shrine|ja|末社}}) of {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}, but during the periodic rebuilding it played a regular role in constructing the "Asame Hall". Furthermore, according to the ''{{ill|Tone River Illustrated History|ja|利根川図志}}'', this shrine was once part of the former territory of a chief priestly family, and its enshrined deity, {{ill|Tennichibune no Mikoto|ja|天鳥船命}}, is regarded as the messenger dispatched under the command of {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神}} when {{ill|Keizutsushi no Kami|ja|経津主神}} of {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}} and {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|鹿島神宮|lt=Takemikazuchi no Kami|lt_ja=武甕槌神}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} negotiated the {{ill|Land Concession|ja|葦原中国平定|lt=transfer of sovereignty|lt_ja=国譲り}} with Ōkuninushi. It is also noted that the Tennichibune, a ship upon which deities ride ({{ill|Ship|ja|諸手船|lt=Various-hand ship|lt_ja=船}}), is alternatively known as Tori no Ishikusu-fune no Kami, while this shrine possesses a large {{ill|Kusunoki|ja|クスノキ}}, commonly called the {{ill|Nanjamonja|ja|ナンジャモンジャ}} tree.{{ill|Mito Kōmon|ja|水戸黄門}} is said to have once pondered, "What on earth is the name of this tree?" Although he did not actually travel throughout Japan, he is known to have visited this shrine. The main tree is surrounded by five saplings; its trunk has a circumference of 13.1 meters and a height of 19 meters, and it is designated as a national {{ill|Natural Monument|ja|天然記念物}} under the name {{ill|Great Kozaki Kusunoki|ja|神崎の大クス}}. The shrine grove, known as the "Kozaki Forest", is designated as a natural monument of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Natural Monuments of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#天然記念物|lt=Chiba Prefecture Natural Monuments|lt_ja=千葉県の天然記念物}}). [[File:Kōzaki-jinja_(Kōzaki,_Chiba)_12.jpg|thumb|right|230px|{{ill|Great Kozaki Kusunoki|ja|神崎の大クス}}. Commonly known as the Nanjamonja tree.]] The shrine has long received strong patronage from successive rulers, and it is said that during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|Sengoku period}} it possessed shrine domains covering 700 {{ill|Town (unit)|ja|町}} ({{ill|Shrine domain|ja|寺社領}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, it held 20 koku of {{ill|Shuin land|ja|朱印地・黒印地|lt=Red Seal Land|lt_ja=朱印領}}, and the "Kōzaki Shrine Documents"—a collection of ancient manuscripts and illustrations passed down from the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} to the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}—is considered an extremely important historical source of the {{ill|Medieval|ja|中世}} period ({{ill|Historical source|ja|史料}}), having been designated as a tangible cultural property (ancient documents) of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Tangible Cultural Property (Ancient Documents) of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#古文書|lt=Chiba Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property (Ancient Documents)|lt_ja=千葉県の有形文化財（古文書）}}). Furthermore, the "Jinguji Documents" held by the former head temple ({{ill|Head temple|ja|別当寺}}) {{ill|Jinguji|ja|神宮寺 (千葉県神崎町)|lt=Jinguji|lt_ja=神宮寺}}, despite missing 60 out of 600 volumes of the {{ill|Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra|ja|大般若波羅蜜多経}}, are highly valued as a sutra transcription from the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|lt=Nanboku-chō period|lt_ja=南北朝期}}. The colophon ({{ill|Colophon|ja|奥書}}) reveals details about the temples ({{ill|Temple|ja|寺院}}), monks ({{ill|Monk|ja|僧侶}}), and the sphere of religious influence at that time, and it has also been designated as a tangible cultural property of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Tangible Cultural Property of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#古文書|lt=Chiba Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property|lt_ja=千葉県の有形文化財}}). == Transportation == * About a 20‐minute walk from {{ill|Narita Line|ja|成田線}}{{ill|Shimōsa-Kozaki Station|ja|下総神崎駅}} * Near the south end of Kozaki Ōhashi on {{ill|National Route 356|ja|国道356号}} and {{ill|Ibaraki and Chiba Prefectural Road 107, Edosaki-Kozaki Line|ja|茨城県道・千葉県道107号江戸崎神崎線}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Kenichi Tanikawa|ja|谷川健一}} ''The Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – Volume 11: Kanto'' {{ill|Hakusuisha|ja|白水社}}, 1984. ISBN 4-560-02221-6 == See Also! == {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|Kozaki Manor|ja|神崎荘}} == External Links == * [http://www.town.kozaki.chiba.jp/05kanko/history.html Kozaki Town History and Cultural Properties] * [http://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/7528/ Chiba Tourism Complete Guide: Great Kozaki Kusunoki (Kozaki Town)] {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Kozaki Town]] [[Category:History of Kozaki Town]] [[Category:Shrines of National Historic Significance]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:神崎神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]]

ISBN 4-560-02221-6 == See Also == {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|Kozaki Manor|ja|神崎荘}} == External Links == * [http://www.town.kozaki.chiba.jp/05kanko/history.html Kozaki Town History and Cultural Properties] * [http://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/7528/ Chiba Tourism Complete Guide: Great Kozaki Kusunoki (Kozaki Town)] {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Kozaki Town]] [[Category:History of Kozaki Town]] [[Category:Shrines of National Historic Significance]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:神崎神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]]

It enshrines as its main deities {{ill|Tennichibune no Mikoto|ja|天鳥船命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|lt=Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|lt_ja=大己貴命}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|スクナビコナ|lt=Sukunahikona no Mikoto|lt_ja=少彦名命}}. == Overview == The founding date of this shrine is unknown, but according to its tradition, it is said that in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Hakuhō|ja|白鳳}} era ({{ill|673|ja|673}}) it was relocated to this site from the two mounds at Ōura Pond, located on the border between {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国|lt=Hitachi|lt_ja=常陸}} and Shimōsa. Until the {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} period, it was known as "Komatsu Shrine", but in the 3rd year of the {{ill|Genkei|ja|元慶}} era ({{ill|879|ja|879}}) it was conferred the lower Junior Fifth Rank ({{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}}下) in the {{ill|Shinto ranking system|ja|神階}}.In the ''{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}'', in the entry for April 5 in the 3rd year of the Genkei era, it is recorded: "To the {{ill|Mihosu Shrine|ja|御穂神社|lt=Miryōjin|lt_ja=御廊神}} of Suruga Province with the upper Junior Fifth Rank, the lower Senior Fifth Rank is conferred, and to the Komatsu Shrine of Shimōsa Province with the upper Sixth Rank, the lower Junior Fifth Rank is conferred." This shrine is not an auxiliary shrine ({{ill|Auxiliary shrine|ja|末社}}) of {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}, but during the periodic rebuilding it played a regular role in constructing the "Asame Hall". Furthermore, according to the ''{{ill|Tone River Illustrated History|ja|利根川図志}}'', this shrine was once part of the former territory of a chief priestly family, and its enshrined deity, {{ill|Tennichibune no Mikoto|ja|天鳥船命}}, is regarded as the messenger dispatched under the command of {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神}} when {{ill|Keizutsushi no Kami|ja|経津主神}} of {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}} and {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|鹿島神宮|lt=Takemikazuchi no Kami|lt_ja=武甕槌神}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} negotiated the {{ill|Land Concession|ja|葦原中国平定|lt=transfer of sovereignty|lt_ja=国譲り}} with Ōkuninushi. It is also noted that the Tennichibune, a ship upon which deities ride ({{ill|Ship|ja|諸手船|lt=Various-hand ship|lt_ja=船}}), is alternatively known as Tori no Ishikusu-fune no Kami, while this shrine possesses a large {{ill|Kusunoki|ja|クスノキ}}, commonly called the {{ill|Nanjamonja|ja|ナンジャモンジャ}} tree.{{ill|Mito Kōmon|ja|水戸黄門}} is said to have once pondered, "What on earth is the name of this tree?" Although he did not actually travel throughout Japan, he is known to have visited this shrine. The main tree is surrounded by five saplings; its trunk has a circumference of 13.1 meters and a height of 19 meters, and it is designated as a national {{ill|Natural Monument|ja|天然記念物}} under the name {{ill|Great Kozaki Kusunoki|ja|神崎の大クス}}. The shrine grove, known as the "Kozaki Forest", is designated as a natural monument of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Natural Monuments of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#天然記念物|lt=Chiba Prefecture Natural Monuments|lt_ja=千葉県の天然記念物}}). [[File:Kōzaki-jinja_(Kōzaki,_Chiba)_12.jpg|thumb|right|230px|{{ill|Great Kozaki Kusunoki|ja|神崎の大クス}}. Commonly known as the Nanjamonja tree.]] The shrine has long received strong patronage from successive rulers, and it is said that during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|Sengoku period}} it possessed shrine domains covering 700 {{ill|Town (unit)|ja|町}} ({{ill|Shrine domain|ja|寺社領}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, it held 20 koku of {{ill|Shuin land|ja|朱印地・黒印地|lt=Red Seal Land|lt_ja=朱印領}}, and the "Kōzaki Shrine Documents"—a collection of ancient manuscripts and illustrations passed down from the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} to the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}—is considered an extremely important historical source of the {{ill|Medieval|ja|中世}} period ({{ill|Historical source|ja|史料}}), having been designated as a tangible cultural property (ancient documents) of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Tangible Cultural Property (Ancient Documents) of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#古文書|lt=Chiba Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property (Ancient Documents)|lt_ja=千葉県の有形文化財（古文書）}}). Furthermore, the "Jinguji Documents" held by the former head temple ({{ill|Head temple|ja|別当寺}}) {{ill|Jinguji|ja|神宮寺 (千葉県神崎町)|lt=Jinguji|lt_ja=神宮寺}}, despite missing 60 out of 600 volumes of the {{ill|Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra|ja|大般若波羅蜜多経}}, are highly valued as a sutra transcription from the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|lt=Nanboku-chō period|lt_ja=南北朝期}}. The colophon ({{ill|Colophon|ja|奥書}}) reveals details about the temples ({{ill|Temple|ja|寺院}}), monks ({{ill|Monk|ja|僧侶}}), and the sphere of religious influence at that time, and it has also been designated as a tangible cultural property of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Tangible Cultural Property of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#古文書|lt=Chiba Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property|lt_ja=千葉県の有形文化財}}). == Transportation == * About a 20‐minute walk from {{ill|Narita Line|ja|成田線}}{{ill|Shimōsa-Kozaki Station|ja|下総神崎駅}} * Near the south end of Kozaki Ōhashi on {{ill|National Route 356|ja|国道356号}} and {{ill|Ibaraki and Chiba Prefectural Road 107, Edosaki-Kozaki Line|ja|茨城県道・千葉県道107号江戸崎神崎線}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Kenichi Tanikawa|ja|谷川健一}} ''The Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – Volume 11: Kanto'' {{ill|Hakusuisha|ja|白水社}}, 1984. ISBN 4-560-02221-6 == See Also == {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|Kozaki Manor|ja|神崎荘}} == External Links == * [http://www.town.kozaki.chiba.jp/05kanko/history.html Kozaki Town History and Cultural Properties] * [http://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/7528/ Chiba Tourism Complete Guide: Great Kozaki Kusunoki (Kozaki Town)] {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Kozaki Town]] [[Category:History of Kozaki Town]] [[Category:Shrines of National Historic Significance]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:神崎神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]]

Until the {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} period, it was known as "Komatsu Shrine", but in the 3rd year of the {{ill|Genkei|ja|元慶}} era ({{ill|879|ja|879}}) it was conferred the lower Junior Fifth Rank ({{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}}下) in the {{ill|Shinto ranking system|ja|神階}}.In the ''{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}'', in the entry for April 5 in the 3rd year of the Genkei era, it is recorded: "To the {{ill|Mihosu Shrine|ja|御穂神社|lt=Miryōjin|lt_ja=御廊神}} of Suruga Province with the upper Junior Fifth Rank, the lower Senior Fifth Rank is conferred, and to the Komatsu Shrine of Shimōsa Province with the upper Sixth Rank, the lower Junior Fifth Rank is conferred." This shrine is not an auxiliary shrine ({{ill|Auxiliary shrine|ja|末社}}) of {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}, but during the periodic rebuilding it played a regular role in constructing the "Asame Hall". Furthermore, according to the ''{{ill|Tone River Illustrated History|ja|利根川図志}}'', this shrine was once part of the former territory of a chief priestly family, and its enshrined deity, {{ill|Tennichibune no Mikoto|ja|天鳥船命}}, is regarded as the messenger dispatched under the command of {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神}} when {{ill|Keizutsushi no Kami|ja|経津主神}} of {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}} and {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|鹿島神宮|lt=Takemikazuchi no Kami|lt_ja=武甕槌神}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} negotiated the {{ill|Land Concession|ja|葦原中国平定|lt=transfer of sovereignty|lt_ja=国譲り}} with Ōkuninushi. It is also noted that the Tennichibune, a ship upon which deities ride ({{ill|Ship|ja|諸手船|lt=Various-hand ship|lt_ja=船}}), is alternatively known as Tori no Ishikusu-fune no Kami, while this shrine possesses a large {{ill|Kusunoki|ja|クスノキ}}, commonly called the {{ill|Nanjamonja|ja|ナンジャモンジャ}} tree.{{ill|Mito Kōmon|ja|水戸黄門}} is said to have once pondered, "What on earth is the name of this tree?" [[File:Kōzaki-jinja_(Kōzaki,_Chiba)_12.jpg|thumb|right|230px|{{ill|Great Kozaki Kusunoki|ja|神崎の大クス}}. ISBN 4-560-02221-6 == See Also == {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|Kozaki Manor|ja|神崎荘}} == External Links == * [http://www.town.kozaki.chiba.jp/05kanko/history.html Kozaki Town History and Cultural Properties] * [http://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/7528/ Chiba Tourism Complete Guide: Great Kozaki Kusunoki (Kozaki Town)] {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Kozaki Town]] [[Category:History of Kozaki Town]] [[Category:Shrines of National Historic Significance]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:神崎神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]][[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]][[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]][[Category:qq]]

During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, it held 20 koku of {{ill|Shuin land|ja|朱印地・黒印地|lt=Red Seal Land|lt_ja=朱印領}}, and the "Kōzaki Shrine Documents"—a collection of ancient manuscripts and illustrations passed down from the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} to the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}—is considered an extremely important historical source of the {{ill|Medieval|ja|中世}} period ({{ill|Historical source|ja|史料}}), having been designated as a tangible cultural property (ancient documents) of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Tangible Cultural Property (Ancient Documents) of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#古文書|lt=Chiba Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property (Ancient Documents)|lt_ja=千葉県の有形文化財（古文書）}}). Furthermore, the "Jinguji Documents" held by the former head temple ({{ill|Head temple|ja|別当寺}}) {{ill|Jinguji|ja|神宮寺 (千葉県神崎町)|lt=Jinguji|lt_ja=神宮寺}}, despite missing 60 out of 600 volumes of the {{ill|Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra|ja|大般若波羅蜜多経}}, are highly valued as a sutra transcription from the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|lt=Nanboku-chō period|lt_ja=南北朝期}}. The colophon ({{ill|Colophon|ja|奥書}}) reveals details about the temples ({{ill|Temple|ja|寺院}}), monks ({{ill|Monk|ja|僧侶}}), and the sphere of religious influence at that time, and it has also been designated as a tangible cultural property of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Tangible Cultural Property of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#古文書|lt=Chiba Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property|lt_ja=千葉県の有形文化財}}). == Transportation == * About a 20‐minute walk from {{ill|Narita Line|ja|成田線}}{{ill|Shimōsa-Kozaki Station|ja|下総神崎駅}} * Near the south end of Kozaki Ōhashi on {{ill|National Route 356|ja|国道356号}} and {{ill|Ibaraki and Chiba Prefectural Road 107, Edosaki-Kozaki Line|ja|茨城県道・千葉県道107号江戸崎神崎線}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Kenichi Tanikawa|ja|谷川健一}} ''The Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – Volume 11: Kanto'' {{ill|Hakusuisha|ja|白水社}}, 1984. ISBN 4-560-02221-6 == See Also == {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|Kozaki Manor|ja|神崎荘}} == External Links == * [http://www.town.kozaki.chiba.jp/05kanko/history.html Kozaki Town History and Cultural Properties] * [http://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/7528/ Chiba Tourism Complete Guide: Great Kozaki Kusunoki (Kozaki Town)] {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Kozaki Town]] [[Category:History of Kozaki Town]] [[Category:Shrines of National Historic Significance]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:神崎神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]][[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]][[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

revision.text:
{{ill|Kōzaki Shrine|ja|神崎神社}} [[Category:Translated Shrines and Temples]] [[Category:Shortest 250 Pages]] [[Category:As of 2025-02-19]] [[sn:ōzaki Shrine]] [[en:Kōzaki Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]] [[sn:ōzaki Shrine]] [[Category:11]] {{Otheruses|Kōzaki Shrine in Chiba Prefecture|Shrine in Kotoura, Tottori Prefecture|Kōzaki Shrine (Kotoura)}} {{infobox Shinto shrine |名称= Kōzaki Shrine |image=[[File:2024-kasai_Kozaki_Shrine_and_Kozaki_no_O-kusu_Katori-JPN.jpg|250px|{{ill|Kōzaki Shrine|ja|神崎神社}}
Worship Hall and the Nanjamonja tree]] |所在地= {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}{{ill|Katori District|ja|香取郡}}{{ill|Kozaki Town|ja|神崎町}} Kozaki Honjuku 1994 |位置= {{coord|35|54|20|N|140|24|0|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |祭神= {{ill|Tennichibune no Mikoto|ja|天鳥船命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|lt=Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|lt_ja=大己貴命}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|スクナビコナ|lt=Sukunahikona no Mikoto|lt_ja=少彦名命}} |社格= Prefectural shrine |創建= |本殿= {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造}} |別名= Komatsu Shrine |例祭= |神事= }} '''Kōzaki Shrine''' (神崎神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}{{ill|Katori District|ja|香取郡}}{{ill|Kozaki Town|ja|神崎町}} ({{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}}{{ill|Katori District|ja|香取郡}}). Its former {{ill|Shrine rank|ja|社格}} was defined under the {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度#諸社|lt=Prefectural shrine|lt_ja=県社}}. It enshrines as its main deities {{ill|Tennichibune no Mikoto|ja|天鳥船命}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|lt=Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|lt_ja=大己貴命}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|スクナビコナ|lt=Sukunahikona no Mikoto|lt_ja=少彦名命}}. == Overview == The founding date of this shrine is unknown, but according to its tradition, it is said that in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Hakuhō|ja|白鳳}} era ({{ill|673|ja|673}}) it was relocated to this site from the two mounds at Ōura Pond, located on the border between {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国|lt=Hitachi|lt_ja=常陸}} and Shimōsa. Until the {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} period, it was known as "Komatsu Shrine", but in the 3rd year of the {{ill|Genkei|ja|元慶}} era ({{ill|879|ja|879}}) it was conferred the lower Junior Fifth Rank ({{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位}}下) in the {{ill|Shinto ranking system|ja|神階}}.In the ''{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}'', in the entry for April 5 in the 3rd year of the Genkei era, it is recorded: "To the {{ill|Mihosu Shrine|ja|御穂神社|lt=Miryōjin|lt_ja=御廊神}} of Suruga Province with the upper Junior Fifth Rank, the lower Senior Fifth Rank is conferred, and to the Komatsu Shrine of Shimōsa Province with the upper Sixth Rank, the lower Junior Fifth Rank is conferred." This shrine is not an auxiliary shrine ({{ill|Auxiliary shrine|ja|末社}}) of {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}}, but during the periodic rebuilding it played a regular role in constructing the "Asame Hall". Furthermore, according to the ''{{ill|Tone River Illustrated History|ja|利根川図志}}'', this shrine was once part of the former territory of a chief priestly family, and its enshrined deity, {{ill|Tennichibune no Mikoto|ja|天鳥船命}}, is regarded as the messenger dispatched under the command of {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神}} when {{ill|Keizutsushi no Kami|ja|経津主神}} of {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮}} and {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|鹿島神宮|lt=Takemikazuchi no Kami|lt_ja=武甕槌神}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} negotiated the {{ill|Land Concession|ja|葦原中国平定|lt=transfer of sovereignty|lt_ja=国譲り}} with Ōkuninushi. It is also noted that the Tennichibune, a ship upon which deities ride ({{ill|Ship|ja|諸手船|lt=Various-hand ship|lt_ja=船}}), is alternatively known as Tori no Ishikusu-fune no Kami, while this shrine possesses a large {{ill|Kusunoki|ja|クスノキ}}, commonly called the {{ill|Nanjamonja|ja|ナンジャモンジャ}} tree.{{ill|Mito Kōmon|ja|水戸黄門}} is said to have once pondered, "What on earth is the name of this tree?" Although he did not actually travel throughout Japan, he is known to have visited this shrine. The main tree is surrounded by five saplings; its trunk has a circumference of 13.1 meters and a height of 19 meters, and it is designated as a national {{ill|Natural Monument|ja|天然記念物}} under the name {{ill|Great Kozaki Kusunoki|ja|神崎の大クス}}. The shrine grove, known as the "Kozaki Forest", is designated as a natural monument of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Natural Monuments of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#天然記念物|lt=Chiba Prefecture Natural Monuments|lt_ja=千葉県の天然記念物}}). [[File:Kōzaki-jinja_(Kōzaki,_Chiba)_12.jpg|thumb|right|230px|{{ill|Great Kozaki Kusunoki|ja|神崎の大クス}}. Commonly known as the Nanjamonja tree.]] The shrine has long received strong patronage from successive rulers, and it is said that during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|Sengoku period}} it possessed shrine domains covering 700 {{ill|Town (unit)|ja|町}} ({{ill|Shrine domain|ja|寺社領}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, it held 20 koku of {{ill|Shuin land|ja|朱印地・黒印地|lt=Red Seal Land|lt_ja=朱印領}}, and the "Kōzaki Shrine Documents"—a collection of ancient manuscripts and illustrations passed down from the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} to the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}—is considered an extremely important historical source of the {{ill|Medieval|ja|中世}} period ({{ill|Historical source|ja|史料}}), having been designated as a tangible cultural property (ancient documents) of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Tangible Cultural Property (Ancient Documents) of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#古文書|lt=Chiba Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property (Ancient Documents)|lt_ja=千葉県の有形文化財（古文書）}}). Furthermore, the "Jinguji Documents" held by the former head temple ({{ill|Head temple|ja|別当寺}}) {{ill|Jinguji|ja|神宮寺 (千葉県神崎町)|lt=Jinguji|lt_ja=神宮寺}}, despite missing 60 out of 600 volumes of the {{ill|Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra|ja|大般若波羅蜜多経}}, are highly valued as a sutra transcription from the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|lt=Nanboku-chō period|lt_ja=南北朝期}}. The colophon ({{ill|Colophon|ja|奥書}}) reveals details about the temples ({{ill|Temple|ja|寺院}}), monks ({{ill|Monk|ja|僧侶}}), and the sphere of religious influence at that time, and it has also been designated as a tangible cultural property of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Tangible Cultural Property of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#古文書|lt=Chiba Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property|lt_ja=千葉県の有形文化財}}). == Transportation == * About a 20‐minute walk from {{ill|Narita Line|ja|成田線}}{{ill|Shimōsa-Kozaki Station|ja|下総神崎駅}} * Near the south end of Kozaki Ōhashi on {{ill|National Route 356|ja|国道356号}} and {{ill|Ibaraki and Chiba Prefectural Road 107, Edosaki-Kozaki Line|ja|茨城県道・千葉県道107号江戸崎神崎線}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Kenichi Tanikawa|ja|谷川健一}} ''The Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – Volume 11: Kanto'' {{ill|Hakusuisha|ja|白水社}}, 1984. ISBN 4-560-02221-6 == See Also == {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|Kozaki Manor|ja|神崎荘}} == External Links == * [http://www.town.kozaki.chiba.jp/05kanko/history.html Kozaki Town History and Cultural Properties] * [http://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/7528/ Chiba Tourism Complete Guide: Great Kozaki Kusunoki (Kozaki Town)] {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Kozaki Town]] [[Category:History of Kozaki Town]] [[Category:Shrines of National Historic Significance]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:神崎神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]][[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]][[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

Worship Hall and the Nanjamonja tree]] |所在地= {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県|12=draft}}{{ill|Katori District|ja|香取郡|12=draft}}{{ill|Kozaki Town|ja|神崎町|12=draft}} Kozaki Honjuku 1994 |位置= {{coord|35|54|20|N|140|24|0|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |祭神= {{ill|Tennichibune no Mikoto|ja|天鳥船命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|lt=Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|lt_ja=大己貴命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|スクナビコナ|lt=Sukunahikona no Mikoto|lt_ja=少彦名命|12=draft}} |社格= Prefectural shrine |創建= |本殿= {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|12=draft}} |別名= Komatsu Shrine |例祭= |神事= }} '''Kōzaki Shrine''' (神崎神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県|12=draft}}{{ill|Katori District|ja|香取郡|12=draft}}{{ill|Kozaki Town|ja|神崎町|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|12=draft}}{{ill|Katori District|ja|香取郡|12=draft}}). Its former {{ill|Shrine rank|ja|社格|12=draft}} was defined under the {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度#諸社|lt=Prefectural shrine|lt_ja=県社|12=draft}}. It enshrines as its main deities {{ill|Tennichibune no Mikoto|ja|天鳥船命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|lt=Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|lt_ja=大己貴命|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|スクナビコナ|lt=Sukunahikona no Mikoto|lt_ja=少彦名命|12=draft}}. == Overview == The founding date of this shrine is unknown, but according to its tradition, it is said that in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Hakuhō|ja|白鳳|12=draft}} era ({{ill|673|ja|673|12=draft}}) it was relocated to this site from the two mounds at Ōura Pond, located on the border between {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国|lt=Hitachi|lt_ja=常陸|12=draft}} and Shimōsa. Until the {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|12=draft}} period, it was known as "Komatsu Shrine", but in the 3rd year of the {{ill|Genkei|ja|元慶|12=draft}} era ({{ill|879|ja|879|12=draft}}) it was conferred the lower Junior Fifth Rank ({{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|12=draft}}下) in the {{ill|Shinto ranking system|ja|神階|12=draft}}.In the ''{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|12=draft}}'', in the entry for April 5 in the 3rd year of the Genkei era, it is recorded: "To the {{ill|Mihosu Shrine|ja|御穂神社|lt=Miryōjin|lt_ja=御廊神|12=draft}} of Suruga Province with the upper Junior Fifth Rank, the lower Senior Fifth Rank is conferred, and to the Komatsu Shrine of Shimōsa Province with the upper Sixth Rank, the lower Junior Fifth Rank is conferred." This shrine is not an auxiliary shrine ({{ill|Auxiliary shrine|ja|末社|12=draft}}) of {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮|12=draft}}, but during the periodic rebuilding it played a regular role in constructing the "Asame Hall". Furthermore, according to the ''{{ill|Tone River Illustrated History|ja|利根川図志|12=draft}}'', this shrine was once part of the former territory of a chief priestly family, and its enshrined deity, {{ill|Tennichibune no Mikoto|ja|天鳥船命|12=draft}}, is regarded as the messenger dispatched under the command of {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|12=draft}} when {{ill|Keizutsushi no Kami|ja|経津主神|12=draft}} of {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮|12=draft}} and {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|鹿島神宮|lt=Takemikazuchi no Kami|lt_ja=武甕槌神|12=draft}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮|12=draft}} negotiated the {{ill|Land Concession|ja|葦原中国平定|lt=transfer of sovereignty|lt_ja=国譲り|12=draft}} with Ōkuninushi. It is also noted that the Tennichibune, a ship upon which deities ride ({{ill|Ship|ja|諸手船|lt=Various-hand ship|lt_ja=船|12=draft}}), is alternatively known as Tori no Ishikusu-fune no Kami, while this shrine possesses a large {{ill|Kusunoki|ja|クスノキ|12=draft}}, commonly called the {{ill|Nanjamonja|ja|ナンジャモンジャ|12=draft}} tree.{{ill|Mito Kōmon|ja|水戸黄門|12=draft}} is said to have once pondered, "What on earth is the name of this tree?" Although he did not actually travel throughout Japan, he is known to have visited this shrine. The main tree is surrounded by five saplings; its trunk has a circumference of 13.1 meters and a height of 19 meters, and it is designated as a national {{ill|Natural Monument|ja|天然記念物|12=draft}} under the name {{ill|Great Kozaki Kusunoki|ja|神崎の大クス|12=draft}}. The shrine grove, known as the "Kozaki Forest", is designated as a natural monument of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Natural Monuments of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#天然記念物|lt=Chiba Prefecture Natural Monuments|lt_ja=千葉県の天然記念物|12=draft}}). [[File:Kōzaki-jinja_(Kōzaki,_Chiba)_12.jpg|thumb|right|230px|{{ill|Great Kozaki Kusunoki|ja|神崎の大クス|12=draft}}. Commonly known as the Nanjamonja tree.]] The shrine has long received strong patronage from successive rulers, and it is said that during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|Sengoku period|12=draft}} it possessed shrine domains covering 700 {{ill|Town (unit)|ja|町|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shrine domain|ja|寺社領|12=draft}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, it held 20 koku of {{ill|Shuin land|ja|朱印地・黒印地|lt=Red Seal Land|lt_ja=朱印領|12=draft}}, and the "Kōzaki Shrine Documents"—a collection of ancient manuscripts and illustrations passed down from the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}} to the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}—is considered an extremely important historical source of the {{ill|Medieval|ja|中世|12=draft}} period ({{ill|Historical source|ja|史料|12=draft}}), having been designated as a tangible cultural property (ancient documents) of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Tangible Cultural Property (Ancient Documents) of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#古文書|lt=Chiba Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property (Ancient Documents)|lt_ja=千葉県の有形文化財（古文書）|12=draft}}). Furthermore, the "Jinguji Documents" held by the former head temple ({{ill|Head temple|ja|別当寺|12=draft}}) {{ill|Jinguji|ja|神宮寺 (千葉県神崎町)|lt=Jinguji|lt_ja=神宮寺|12=draft}}, despite missing 60 out of 600 volumes of the {{ill|Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra|ja|大般若波羅蜜多経|12=draft}}, are highly valued as a sutra transcription from the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|lt=Nanboku-chō period|lt_ja=南北朝期|12=draft}}. The colophon ({{ill|Colophon|ja|奥書|12=draft}}) reveals details about the temples ({{ill|Temple|ja|寺院|12=draft}}), monks ({{ill|Monk|ja|僧侶|12=draft}}), and the sphere of religious influence at that time, and it has also been designated as a tangible cultural property of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Tangible Cultural Property of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#古文書|lt=Chiba Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property|lt_ja=千葉県の有形文化財|12=draft}}). == Transportation == * About a 20‐minute walk from {{ill|Narita Line|ja|成田線|12=draft}}{{ill|Shimōsa-Kozaki Station|ja|下総神崎駅|12=draft}} * Near the south end of Kozaki Ōhashi on {{ill|National Route 356|ja|国道356号|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ibaraki and Chiba Prefectural Road 107, Edosaki-Kozaki Line|ja|茨城県道・千葉県道107号江戸崎神崎線|12=draft}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Kenichi Tanikawa|ja|谷川健一|12=draft}} ''The Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – Volume 11: Kanto'' {{ill|Hakusuisha|ja|白水社|12=draft}}, 1984. ISBN 4-560-02221-6 == See Also == {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|Kozaki Manor|ja|神崎荘|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.town.kozaki.chiba.jp/05kanko/history.html Kozaki Town History and Cultural Properties] * [http://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/7528/ Chiba Tourism Complete Guide: Great Kozaki Kusunoki (Kozaki Town)] {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Translated Shrines and Temples]] [[Category:Shortest 250 Pages]] [[Category:As of 2025-02-19]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Kozaki Town]] [[Category:History of Kozaki Town]] [[Category:Shrines of National Historic Significance]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:ōzaki Shrine]] [[en:Kōzaki Shrine]] [[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]] [[ja:神崎神社]]

[[File:Kōzaki-jinja_(Kōzaki,_Chiba)_12.jpg|thumb|right|230px|{{ill|Great Kozaki Kusunoki|ja|神崎の大クス|12=draft}}. Commonly known as the Nanjamonja tree.]] The shrine has long received strong patronage from successive rulers, and it is said that during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|Sengoku period|12=draft}} it possessed shrine domains covering 700 {{ill|Town (unit)|ja|町|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shrine domain|ja|寺社領|12=draft}}). Furthermore, the "Jinguji Documents" held by the former head temple ({{ill|Head temple|ja|別当寺|12=draft}}) {{ill|Jinguji|ja|神宮寺 (千葉県神崎町)|lt=Jinguji|lt_ja=神宮寺|12=draft}}, despite missing 60 out of 600 volumes of the {{ill|Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra|ja|大般若波羅蜜多経|12=draft}}, are highly valued as a sutra transcription from the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|lt=Nanboku-chō period|lt_ja=南北朝期|12=draft}}. This shrine is not an auxiliary shrine ({{ill|Auxiliary shrine|ja|末社|12=draft}}) of {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮|12=draft}}, but during the periodic rebuilding it played a regular role in constructing the "Asame Hall". Furthermore, according to the ''{{ill|Tone River Illustrated History|ja|利根川図志|12=draft}}'', this shrine was once part of the former territory of a chief priestly family, and its enshrined deity, {{ill|Tennichibune no Mikoto|ja|天鳥船命|12=draft}}, is regarded as the messenger dispatched under the command of {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|12=draft}} when {{ill|Keizutsushi no Kami|ja|経津主神|12=draft}} of {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮|12=draft}} and {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|鹿島神宮|lt=Takemikazuchi no Kami|lt_ja=武甕槌神|12=draft}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮|12=draft}} negotiated the {{ill|Land Concession|ja|葦原中国平定|lt=transfer of sovereignty|lt_ja=国譲り|12=draft}} with Ōkuninushi. It is also noted that the Tennichibune, a ship upon which deities ride ({{ill|Ship|ja|諸手船|lt=Various-hand ship|lt_ja=船|12=draft}}), is alternatively known as Tori no Ishikusu-fune no Kami, while this shrine possesses a large {{ill|Kusunoki|ja|クスノキ|12=draft}}, commonly called the {{ill|Nanjamonja|ja|ナンジャモンジャ|12=draft}} tree.{{ill|Mito Kōmon|ja|水戸黄門|12=draft}} is said to have once pondered, "What on earth is the name of this tree?" Although he did not actually travel throughout Japan, he is known to have visited this shrine. The main tree is surrounded by five saplings; its trunk has a circumference of 13.1 meters and a height of 19 meters, and it is designated as a national {{ill|Natural Monument|ja|天然記念物|12=draft}} under the name {{ill|Great Kozaki Kusunoki|ja|神崎の大クス|12=draft}}. The shrine grove, known as the "Kozaki Forest", is designated as a natural monument of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Natural Monuments of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#天然記念物|lt=Chiba Prefecture Natural Monuments|lt_ja=千葉県の天然記念物|12=draft}}). [[File:Kōzaki-jinja_(Kōzaki,_Chiba)_12.jpg|thumb|right|230px|{{ill|Great Kozaki Kusunoki|ja|神崎の大クス|12=draft}}. Commonly known as the Nanjamonja tree.]] The shrine has long received strong patronage from successive rulers, and it is said that during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|Sengoku period|12=draft}} it possessed shrine domains covering 700 {{ill|Town (unit)|ja|町|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shrine domain|ja|寺社領|12=draft}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, it held 20 koku of {{ill|Shuin land|ja|朱印地・黒印地|lt=Red Seal Land|lt_ja=朱印領|12=draft}}, and the "Kōzaki Shrine Documents"—a collection of ancient manuscripts and illustrations passed down from the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}} to the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}—is considered an extremely important historical source of the {{ill|Medieval|ja|中世|12=draft}} period ({{ill|Historical source|ja|史料|12=draft}}), having been designated as a tangible cultural property (ancient documents) of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Tangible Cultural Property (Ancient Documents) of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#古文書|lt=Chiba Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property (Ancient Documents)|lt_ja=千葉県の有形文化財（古文書）|12=draft}}). Furthermore, the "Jinguji Documents" held by the former head temple ({{ill|Head temple|ja|別当寺|12=draft}}) {{ill|Jinguji|ja|神宮寺 (千葉県神崎町)|lt=Jinguji|lt_ja=神宮寺|12=draft}}, despite missing 60 out of 600 volumes of the {{ill|Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra|ja|大般若波羅蜜多経|12=draft}}, are highly valued as a sutra transcription from the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|lt=Nanboku-chō period|lt_ja=南北朝期|12=draft}}. The colophon ({{ill|Colophon|ja|奥書|12=draft}}) reveals details about the temples ({{ill|Temple|ja|寺院|12=draft}}), monks ({{ill|Monk|ja|僧侶|12=draft}}), and the sphere of religious influence at that time, and it has also been designated as a tangible cultural property of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Tangible Cultural Property of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#古文書|lt=Chiba Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property|lt_ja=千葉県の有形文化財|12=draft}}). == Transportation == * About a 20‐minute walk from {{ill|Narita Line|ja|成田線|12=draft}}{{ill|Shimōsa-Kozaki Station|ja|下総神崎駅|12=draft}} * Near the south end of Kozaki Ōhashi on {{ill|National Route 356|ja|国道356号|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ibaraki and Chiba Prefectural Road 107, Edosaki-Kozaki Line|ja|茨城県道・千葉県道107号江戸崎神崎線|12=draft}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Kenichi Tanikawa|ja|谷川健一|12=draft}} ''The Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – Volume 11: Kanto'' {{ill|Hakusuisha|ja|白水社|12=draft}}, 1984. ISBN 4-560-02221-6 == See Also == {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|Kozaki Manor|ja|神崎荘|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.town.kozaki.chiba.jp/05kanko/history.html Kozaki Town History and Cultural Properties] * [http://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/7528/ Chiba Tourism Complete Guide: Great Kozaki Kusunoki (Kozaki Town)] {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Translated Shrines and Temples]] [[Category:Shortest 250 Pages]] [[Category:As of 2025-02-19]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Kozaki Town]] [[Category:History of Kozaki Town]] [[Category:Shrines of National Historic Significance]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:ōzaki Shrine]] [[en:Kōzaki Shrine]] [[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]] [[ja:神崎神社]]

revision.text:
{{ill|Kōzaki Shrine|ja|神崎神社|12=draft}} {{Otheruses|Kōzaki Shrine in Chiba Prefecture|Shrine in Kotoura, Tottori Prefecture|Kōzaki Shrine (Kotoura)}} {{infobox Shinto shrine |名称= Kōzaki Shrine |image=[[File:2024-kasai_Kozaki_Shrine_and_Kozaki_no_O-kusu_Katori-JPN.jpg|250px|{{ill|Kōzaki Shrine|ja|神崎神社|12=draft}}
Worship Hall and the Nanjamonja tree]] |所在地= {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県|12=draft}}{{ill|Katori District|ja|香取郡|12=draft}}{{ill|Kozaki Town|ja|神崎町|12=draft}} Kozaki Honjuku 1994 |位置= {{coord|35|54|20|N|140|24|0|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |祭神= {{ill|Tennichibune no Mikoto|ja|天鳥船命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|lt=Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|lt_ja=大己貴命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|スクナビコナ|lt=Sukunahikona no Mikoto|lt_ja=少彦名命|12=draft}} |社格= Prefectural shrine |創建= |本殿= {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|12=draft}} |別名= Komatsu Shrine |例祭= |神事= }} '''Kōzaki Shrine''' (神崎神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県|12=draft}}{{ill|Katori District|ja|香取郡|12=draft}}{{ill|Kozaki Town|ja|神崎町|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|12=draft}}{{ill|Katori District|ja|香取郡|12=draft}}). Its former {{ill|Shrine rank|ja|社格|12=draft}} was defined under the {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度#諸社|lt=Prefectural shrine|lt_ja=県社|12=draft}}. It enshrines as its main deities {{ill|Tennichibune no Mikoto|ja|天鳥船命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|lt=Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|lt_ja=大己貴命|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|スクナビコナ|lt=Sukunahikona no Mikoto|lt_ja=少彦名命|12=draft}}. == Overview == The founding date of this shrine is unknown, but according to its tradition, it is said that in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Hakuhō|ja|白鳳|12=draft}} era ({{ill|673|ja|673|12=draft}}) it was relocated to this site from the two mounds at Ōura Pond, located on the border between {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国|lt=Hitachi|lt_ja=常陸|12=draft}} and Shimōsa. Until the {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|12=draft}} period, it was known as "Komatsu Shrine", but in the 3rd year of the {{ill|Genkei|ja|元慶|12=draft}} era ({{ill|879|ja|879|12=draft}}) it was conferred the lower Junior Fifth Rank ({{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|12=draft}}下) in the {{ill|Shinto ranking system|ja|神階|12=draft}}.In the ''{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|12=draft}}'', in the entry for April 5 in the 3rd year of the Genkei era, it is recorded: "To the {{ill|Mihosu Shrine|ja|御穂神社|lt=Miryōjin|lt_ja=御廊神|12=draft}} of Suruga Province with the upper Junior Fifth Rank, the lower Senior Fifth Rank is conferred, and to the Komatsu Shrine of Shimōsa Province with the upper Sixth Rank, the lower Junior Fifth Rank is conferred." This shrine is not an auxiliary shrine ({{ill|Auxiliary shrine|ja|末社|12=draft}}) of {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮|12=draft}}, but during the periodic rebuilding it played a regular role in constructing the "Asame Hall". Furthermore, according to the ''{{ill|Tone River Illustrated History|ja|利根川図志|12=draft}}'', this shrine was once part of the former territory of a chief priestly family, and its enshrined deity, {{ill|Tennichibune no Mikoto|ja|天鳥船命|12=draft}}, is regarded as the messenger dispatched under the command of {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|12=draft}} when {{ill|Keizutsushi no Kami|ja|経津主神|12=draft}} of {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮|12=draft}} and {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|鹿島神宮|lt=Takemikazuchi no Kami|lt_ja=武甕槌神|12=draft}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮|12=draft}} negotiated the {{ill|Land Concession|ja|葦原中国平定|lt=transfer of sovereignty|lt_ja=国譲り|12=draft}} with Ōkuninushi. It is also noted that the Tennichibune, a ship upon which deities ride ({{ill|Ship|ja|諸手船|lt=Various-hand ship|lt_ja=船|12=draft}}), is alternatively known as Tori no Ishikusu-fune no Kami, while this shrine possesses a large {{ill|Kusunoki|ja|クスノキ|12=draft}}, commonly called the {{ill|Nanjamonja|ja|ナンジャモンジャ|12=draft}} tree.{{ill|Mito Kōmon|ja|水戸黄門|12=draft}} is said to have once pondered, "What on earth is the name of this tree?" Although he did not actually travel throughout Japan, he is known to have visited this shrine. The main tree is surrounded by five saplings; its trunk has a circumference of 13.1 meters and a height of 19 meters, and it is designated as a national {{ill|Natural Monument|ja|天然記念物|12=draft}} under the name {{ill|Great Kozaki Kusunoki|ja|神崎の大クス|12=draft}}. The shrine grove, known as the "Kozaki Forest", is designated as a natural monument of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Natural Monuments of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#天然記念物|lt=Chiba Prefecture Natural Monuments|lt_ja=千葉県の天然記念物|12=draft}}). [[File:Kōzaki-jinja_(Kōzaki,_Chiba)_12.jpg|thumb|right|230px|{{ill|Great Kozaki Kusunoki|ja|神崎の大クス|12=draft}}. Commonly known as the Nanjamonja tree.]] The shrine has long received strong patronage from successive rulers, and it is said that during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|Sengoku period|12=draft}} it possessed shrine domains covering 700 {{ill|Town (unit)|ja|町|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shrine domain|ja|寺社領|12=draft}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, it held 20 koku of {{ill|Shuin land|ja|朱印地・黒印地|lt=Red Seal Land|lt_ja=朱印領|12=draft}}, and the "Kōzaki Shrine Documents"—a collection of ancient manuscripts and illustrations passed down from the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}} to the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}—is considered an extremely important historical source of the {{ill|Medieval|ja|中世|12=draft}} period ({{ill|Historical source|ja|史料|12=draft}}), having been designated as a tangible cultural property (ancient documents) of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Tangible Cultural Property (Ancient Documents) of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#古文書|lt=Chiba Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property (Ancient Documents)|lt_ja=千葉県の有形文化財（古文書）|12=draft}}). Furthermore, the "Jinguji Documents" held by the former head temple ({{ill|Head temple|ja|別当寺|12=draft}}) {{ill|Jinguji|ja|神宮寺 (千葉県神崎町)|lt=Jinguji|lt_ja=神宮寺|12=draft}}, despite missing 60 out of 600 volumes of the {{ill|Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra|ja|大般若波羅蜜多経|12=draft}}, are highly valued as a sutra transcription from the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|lt=Nanboku-chō period|lt_ja=南北朝期|12=draft}}. The colophon ({{ill|Colophon|ja|奥書|12=draft}}) reveals details about the temples ({{ill|Temple|ja|寺院|12=draft}}), monks ({{ill|Monk|ja|僧侶|12=draft}}), and the sphere of religious influence at that time, and it has also been designated as a tangible cultural property of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Tangible Cultural Property of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#古文書|lt=Chiba Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property|lt_ja=千葉県の有形文化財|12=draft}}). == Transportation == * About a 20‐minute walk from {{ill|Narita Line|ja|成田線|12=draft}}{{ill|Shimōsa-Kozaki Station|ja|下総神崎駅|12=draft}} * Near the south end of Kozaki Ōhashi on {{ill|National Route 356|ja|国道356号|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ibaraki and Chiba Prefectural Road 107, Edosaki-Kozaki Line|ja|茨城県道・千葉県道107号江戸崎神崎線|12=draft}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Kenichi Tanikawa|ja|谷川健一|12=draft}} ''The Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – Volume 11: Kanto'' {{ill|Hakusuisha|ja|白水社|12=draft}}, 1984. ISBN 4-560-02221-6 == See Also == {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|Kozaki Manor|ja|神崎荘|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.town.kozaki.chiba.jp/05kanko/history.html Kozaki Town History and Cultural Properties] * [http://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/7528/ Chiba Tourism Complete Guide: Great Kozaki Kusunoki (Kozaki Town)] {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Translated Shrines and Temples]] [[Category:Shortest 250 Pages]] [[Category:As of 2025-02-19]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Kozaki Town]] [[Category:History of Kozaki Town]] [[Category:Shrines of National Historic Significance]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:ōzaki Shrine]] [[en:Kōzaki Shrine]] [[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]] [[ja:神崎神社]]

During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, it held 20 koku of {{ill|Shuin land|ja|朱印地・黒印地|lt=Red Seal Land|lt_ja=朱印領|12=draft}}, and the "Kōzaki Shrine Documents"—a collection of ancient manuscripts and illustrations passed down from the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}} to the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}—is considered an extremely important historical source of the {{ill|Medieval|ja|中世|12=draft}} period ({{ill|Historical source|ja|史料|12=draft}}), having been designated as a tangible cultural property (ancient documents) of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Tangible Cultural Property (Ancient Documents) of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#古文書|lt=Chiba Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property (Ancient Documents)|lt_ja=千葉県の有形文化財（古文書）|12=draft}}). Furthermore, the "Jinguji Documents" held by the former head temple ({{ill|Head temple|ja|別当寺|12=draft}}) {{ill|Jinguji|ja|神宮寺 (千葉県神崎町)|lt=Jinguji|lt_ja=神宮寺|12=draft}}, despite missing 60 out of 600 volumes of the {{ill|Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra|ja|大般若波羅蜜多経|12=draft}}, are highly valued as a sutra transcription from the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|lt=Nanboku-chō period|lt_ja=南北朝期|12=draft}}. The colophon ({{ill|Colophon|ja|奥書|12=draft}}) reveals details about the temples ({{ill|Temple|ja|寺院|12=draft}}), monks ({{ill|Monk|ja|僧侶|12=draft}}), and the sphere of religious influence at that time, and it has also been designated as a tangible cultural property of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Tangible Cultural Property of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#古文書|lt=Chiba Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property|lt_ja=千葉県の有形文化財|12=draft}}). == Transportation == * About a 20‐minute walk from {{ill|Narita Line|ja|成田線|12=draft}}{{ill|Shimōsa-Kozaki Station|ja|下総神崎駅|12=draft}} * Near the south end of Kozaki Ōhashi on {{ill|National Route 356|ja|国道356号|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ibaraki and Chiba Prefectural Road 107, Edosaki-Kozaki Line|ja|茨城県道・千葉県道107号江戸崎神崎線|12=draft}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Kenichi Tanikawa|ja|谷川健一|12=draft}} ''The Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – Volume 11: Kanto'' {{ill|Hakusuisha|ja|白水社|12=draft}}, 1984. ISBN 4-560-02221-6 == See Also == {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|Kozaki Manor|ja|神崎荘|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.town.kozaki.chiba.jp/05kanko/history.html Kozaki Town History and Cultural Properties] * [http://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/7528/ Chiba Tourism Complete Guide: Great Kozaki Kusunoki (Kozaki Town)] {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Translated Shrines and Temples]] [[Category:Shortest 250 Pages]] [[Category:As of 2025-02-19]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Kozaki Town]] [[Category:History of Kozaki Town]] [[Category:Shrines of National Historic Significance]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:ōzaki Shrine]] [[en:Kōzaki Shrine]] [[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]] [[ja:神崎神社]]

revision.text:
{{ill|Kōzaki Shrine|ja|神崎神社|12=draft}} {{Otheruses|Kōzaki Shrine in Chiba Prefecture|Shrine in Kotoura, Tottori Prefecture|Kōzaki Shrine (Kotoura)}} {{infobox Shinto shrine |名称= Kōzaki Shrine |image=[[File:2024-kasai_Kozaki_Shrine_and_Kozaki_no_O-kusu_Katori-JPN.jpg|250px|{{ill|Kōzaki Shrine|ja|神崎神社|12=draft}}
Worship Hall and the Nanjamonja tree]] |所在地= {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県|12=draft}}{{ill|Katori District|ja|香取郡|12=draft}}{{ill|Kozaki Town|ja|神崎町|12=draft}} Kozaki Honjuku 1994 |位置= {{coord|35|54|20|N|140|24|0|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |祭神= {{ill|Tennichibune no Mikoto|ja|天鳥船命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|lt=Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|lt_ja=大己貴命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|スクナビコナ|lt=Sukunahikona no Mikoto|lt_ja=少彦名命|12=draft}} |社格= Prefectural shrine |創建= |本殿= {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|ja|流造|12=draft}} |別名= Komatsu Shrine |例祭= |神事= }} '''Kōzaki Shrine''' (神崎神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県|12=draft}}{{ill|Katori District|ja|香取郡|12=draft}}{{ill|Kozaki Town|ja|神崎町|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国|12=draft}}{{ill|Katori District|ja|香取郡|12=draft}}). Its former {{ill|Shrine rank|ja|社格|12=draft}} was defined under the {{ill|Modern Shrine Ranking System|ja|近代社格制度#諸社|lt=Prefectural shrine|lt_ja=県社|12=draft}}. It enshrines as its main deities {{ill|Tennichibune no Mikoto|ja|天鳥船命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|lt=Ōkuninushi no Mikoto|lt_ja=大己貴命|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|スクナビコナ|lt=Sukunahikona no Mikoto|lt_ja=少彦名命|12=draft}}. == Overview == The founding date of this shrine is unknown, but according to its tradition, it is said that in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Hakuhō|ja|白鳳|12=draft}} era ({{ill|673|ja|673|12=draft}}) it was relocated to this site from the two mounds at Ōura Pond, located on the border between {{ill|Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国|lt=Hitachi|lt_ja=常陸|12=draft}} and Shimōsa. Until the {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|12=draft}} period, it was known as "Komatsu Shrine", but in the 3rd year of the {{ill|Genkei|ja|元慶|12=draft}} era ({{ill|879|ja|879|12=draft}}) it was conferred the lower Junior Fifth Rank ({{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|12=draft}}下) in the {{ill|Shinto ranking system|ja|神階|12=draft}}.In the ''{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|12=draft}}'', in the entry for April 5 in the 3rd year of the Genkei era, it is recorded: "To the {{ill|Mihosu Shrine|ja|御穂神社|lt=Miryōjin|lt_ja=御廊神|12=draft}} of Suruga Province with the upper Junior Fifth Rank, the lower Senior Fifth Rank is conferred, and to the Komatsu Shrine of Shimōsa Province with the upper Sixth Rank, the lower Junior Fifth Rank is conferred." This shrine is not an auxiliary shrine ({{ill|Auxiliary shrine|ja|末社|12=draft}}) of {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮|12=draft}}, but during the periodic rebuilding it played a regular role in constructing the "Asame Hall". Furthermore, according to the ''{{ill|Tone River Illustrated History|ja|利根川図志|12=draft}}'', this shrine was once part of the former territory of a chief priestly family, and its enshrined deity, {{ill|Tennichibune no Mikoto|ja|天鳥船命|12=draft}}, is regarded as the messenger dispatched under the command of {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|12=draft}} when {{ill|Keizutsushi no Kami|ja|経津主神|12=draft}} of {{ill|Katori Shrine|ja|香取神宮|12=draft}} and {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|鹿島神宮|lt=Takemikazuchi no Kami|lt_ja=武甕槌神|12=draft}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮|12=draft}} negotiated the {{ill|Land Concession|ja|葦原中国平定|lt=transfer of sovereignty|lt_ja=国譲り|12=draft}} with Ōkuninushi. It is also noted that the Tennichibune, a ship upon which deities ride ({{ill|Ship|ja|諸手船|lt=Various-hand ship|lt_ja=船|12=draft}}), is alternatively known as Tori no Ishikusu-fune no Kami, while this shrine possesses a large {{ill|Kusunoki|ja|クスノキ|12=draft}}, commonly called the {{ill|Nanjamonja|ja|ナンジャモンジャ|12=draft}} tree.{{ill|Mito Kōmon|ja|水戸黄門|12=draft}} is said to have once pondered, "What on earth is the name of this tree?" Although he did not actually travel throughout Japan, he is known to have visited this shrine. The main tree is surrounded by five saplings; its trunk has a circumference of 13.1 meters and a height of 19 meters, and it is designated as a national {{ill|Natural Monument|ja|天然記念物|12=draft}} under the name {{ill|Great Kozaki Kusunoki|ja|神崎の大クス|12=draft}}. The shrine grove, known as the "Kozaki Forest", is designated as a natural monument of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Natural Monuments of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#天然記念物|lt=Chiba Prefecture Natural Monuments|lt_ja=千葉県の天然記念物|12=draft}}). [[File:Kōzaki-jinja_(Kōzaki,_Chiba)_12.jpg|thumb|right|230px|{{ill|Great Kozaki Kusunoki|ja|神崎の大クス|12=draft}}. Commonly known as the Nanjamonja tree.]] The shrine has long received strong patronage from successive rulers, and it is said that during the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|Sengoku period|12=draft}} it possessed shrine domains covering 700 {{ill|Town (unit)|ja|町|12=draft}} ({{ill|Shrine domain|ja|寺社領|12=draft}}). During the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, it held 20 koku of {{ill|Shuin land|ja|朱印地・黒印地|lt=Red Seal Land|lt_ja=朱印領|12=draft}}, and the "Kōzaki Shrine Documents"—a collection of ancient manuscripts and illustrations passed down from the early {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}} to the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}—is considered an extremely important historical source of the {{ill|Medieval|ja|中世|12=draft}} period ({{ill|Historical source|ja|史料|12=draft}}), having been designated as a tangible cultural property (ancient documents) of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Tangible Cultural Property (Ancient Documents) of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#古文書|lt=Chiba Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property (Ancient Documents)|lt_ja=千葉県の有形文化財（古文書）|12=draft}}). Furthermore, the "Jinguji Documents" held by the former head temple ({{ill|Head temple|ja|別当寺|12=draft}}) {{ill|Jinguji|ja|神宮寺 (千葉県神崎町)|lt=Jinguji|lt_ja=神宮寺|12=draft}}, despite missing 60 out of 600 volumes of the {{ill|Mahaprajnaparamita Sutra|ja|大般若波羅蜜多経|12=draft}}, are highly valued as a sutra transcription from the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代 (日本)|lt=Nanboku-chō period|lt_ja=南北朝期|12=draft}}. The colophon ({{ill|Colophon|ja|奥書|12=draft}}) reveals details about the temples ({{ill|Temple|ja|寺院|12=draft}}), monks ({{ill|Monk|ja|僧侶|12=draft}}), and the sphere of religious influence at that time, and it has also been designated as a tangible cultural property of Chiba Prefecture ({{ill|Tangible Cultural Property of Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県指定文化財一覧#古文書|lt=Chiba Prefecture Tangible Cultural Property|lt_ja=千葉県の有形文化財|12=draft}}). == Transportation == * About a 20‐minute walk from {{ill|Narita Line|ja|成田線|12=draft}}{{ill|Shimōsa-Kozaki Station|ja|下総神崎駅|12=draft}} * Near the south end of Kozaki Ōhashi on {{ill|National Route 356|ja|国道356号|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ibaraki and Chiba Prefectural Road 107, Edosaki-Kozaki Line|ja|茨城県道・千葉県道107号江戸崎神崎線|12=draft}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Kenichi Tanikawa|ja|谷川健一|12=draft}} ''The Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – Volume 11: Kanto'' {{ill|Hakusuisha|ja|白水社|12=draft}}, 1984. ISBN 4-560-02221-6 == See Also == {{Commonscat}} * {{ill|Kozaki Manor|ja|神崎荘|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.town.kozaki.chiba.jp/05kanko/history.html Kozaki Town History and Cultural Properties] * [http://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/7528/ Chiba Tourism Complete Guide: Great Kozaki Kusunoki (Kozaki Town)] {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Translated Shrines and Temples]] [[Category:Shortest 250 Pages]] [[Category:As of 2025-02-19]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Kozaki Town]] [[Category:History of Kozaki Town]] [[Category:Shrines of National Historic Significance]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:ōzaki Shrine]] [[en:Kōzaki Shrine]] [[sn:Kōzaki Shrine]] [[ja:神崎神社]]

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|千葉県にある神崎神社|鳥取県琴浦町にある神社|神崎神社 (琴浦町)}} {{神社 |名称= 神崎神社 |画像=[[File:2024-kasai_Kozaki_Shrine_and_Kozaki_no_O-kusu_Katori-JPN.jpg|250px|神崎神社]]
拝殿とナンジャモンジャの木| |所在地= [[千葉県]][[香取郡]][[神崎町]]神崎本宿1994 |位置= {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|54|20|N|140|24|0|E|region:JP-12_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |祭神= 天鳥船命、大己貴命、少彦名命 |社格= 県社 |創建= |本殿= [[流造]] |別名= 子松神社 |例祭= |神事= }} '''神崎神社'''（こうざきじんじゃ）は、[[千葉県]][[香取郡]][[神崎町]]（[[下総国]][[香取郡]]）にある[[神社]]。旧[[社格]]は[[近代社格制度#諸社|県社]]。 [[天鳥船命]]、[[大国主|大己貴命]]、[[スクナビコナ|少彦名命]]を主祭神とする。 == 概要 == この神社の創建年代については不詳であるが、社伝によると[[白鳳]]2年（[[673年]]）に[[常陸国|常陸]]と下総の境界にある大浦沼の二つ塚からこの地に影向して遷座したものという。[[明治]]期までは「子松神社」と称していたが、[[元慶]]3年（[[879年]]）に[[従五位]]下の[[神階]]を授けられている『[[日本三代実録]]』元慶3年4月5日条に、「[[駿河国]]従五位上の[[御穂神社|御廬神]]に正五位下、下総国正六位上の子松神に従五位下を授ける」とある。。 当社は[[香取神宮]]の[[末社]]ではないが、式年造営のときは「あさめ殿」を造る常役であった。また『[[利根川図志]]』には当社は大禰宜家の旧領であったと記されており、祭神の天鳥船命は、香取神宮の[[経津主神]]と[[鹿島神宮]]の[[タケミカヅチ|武甕槌神]]が大己貴命と[[葦原中国平定|国譲り]]の交渉をした際、[[事代主神]]のもとへ使わされた神とされていることからも、当社と香取神宮の関係が窺える。なお、天鳥船は神が乗る[[諸手船|船]]でもあり天鳥船命は鳥之石楠船神ともいわれるが、当社には[[クスノキ]]の大木があり[[ナンジャモンジャ]]の木と呼ばれている[[水戸黄門]]が、「この木は何というもんじゃろうか」と自問自答したことによるとされている。水戸黄門は実際には日本各地を漫遊したわけではないが、当社には訪れている。。親木を5本のひこばえが取り巻いているもので、主幹の根元は、周囲13.1メートル、樹高は19メートルあり、[[神崎の大クス]]として、国の[[天然記念物]]に指定されている。[[社叢林]]は神崎の森として、[[千葉県指定文化財一覧#天然記念物|千葉県の天然記念物]]に指定されている。 [[File:Kōzaki-jinja_(Kōzaki,_Chiba)_12.jpg|thumb|right|230px|[[神崎の大クス]]。通称ナンジャモンジャの木。]] 当社は古くから歴代支配者による厚い庇護を受け、[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]には700[[町 (単位)|町]]の[[寺社領|社領]]があったといわれ、[[江戸時代]]には[[朱印地・黒印地|朱印領]]20石を有しており、当社に伝わり[[鎌倉時代]]初期から[[室町時代]]に及ぶ古文書・絵図などからなる「神崎神社文書」は[[中世]]の[[史料]]としてきわめて重要なものとされ、[[千葉県指定文化財一覧#古文書|千葉県の有形文化財（古文書）]]に指定されている。さらに当社の[[別当寺]]であった[[神宮寺 (千葉県神崎町)|神宮寺]]が所蔵する「神宮寺文書」は、[[大般若波羅蜜多経]]600巻のうち60巻を欠くものの、[[南北朝時代 (日本)|南北朝期]]の[[写経]]として価値が高く、[[奥書]]から当時の[[寺院]]や[[僧侶]]の様子、また信仰圏などが知られ、同じく千葉県の有形文化財に指定されている。 == 交通 == * [[成田線]][[下総神崎駅]]から徒歩約20分 * [[国道356号]]、[[茨城県道・千葉県道107号江戸崎神崎線]] 神崎大橋南詰近く == 脚注 == {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * [[谷川健一]] 『日本の神々－神社と聖地－第十一巻 関東』 [[白水社]]、1984年。ISBN 4-560-02221-6 == 関連項目 == {{Commonscat}} * [[神崎荘]] == 外部リンク == * [http://www.town.kozaki.chiba.jp/05kanko/history.html 神崎町 歴史・文化財] * [http://maruchiba.jp/sys/data/index/page/id/7528/ ちばの観光まるごと紹介 神崎の大クス（神崎町）] {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:こうさきしんしや}} [[Category:千葉県の神社]] [[Category:神崎町の建築物]] [[Category:神崎町の歴史]] [[Category:国史見在社]] [[Category:千葉県の旧県社]]
